/** * A record batch is a container for one or more {@link Record} objects and is the fundamental unit of * data transfer in the Kafka wire protocol. Producers write batches to partition leaders; brokers * replicate, validate, and store them; consumers fetch and deserialize them. * *

The Kafka record format has evolved across three magic versions: *

    *
  • Magic v0 ({@link #MAGIC_VALUE_V0}) — original message format; no timestamps, no compression * across multiple messages in a single logical unit.
    • *
  • Magic v1 ({@link #MAGIC_VALUE_V1}) — added per-record timestamps and outer-record * compression, but a batch with no compression still contained exactly one record.
    • *
  • Magic v2 ({@link #MAGIC_VALUE_V2}, {@link #CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE}) — redesigned batch * format with a fixed batch header, variable-length delta-encoded records, efficient CRC-32C * checksumming, and first-class support for idempotent and transactional producers.
    • *
* *

Implementations carry read-only access to serialized bytes (e.g. a slice of a * {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer}). Callers should iterate records via {@link #iterator()} for simple * use cases or via {@link #streamingIterator(org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier)} for * memory-efficient streaming decompression. The streaming iterator must be closed after use. * *

Thread safety: Implementations of this interface are not guaranteed to be * thread-safe. {@link DefaultRecordBatch} instances wrap a shared {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer} and * must not be accessed concurrently from multiple threads without external synchronization. * * @see DefaultRecordBatch * @see MemoryRecords * @see Record */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

 *  Length => Int32

 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32

 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16

 *  RecordsCount => Int32

 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for
 * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and
 * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.

 * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty
 * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches
 * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId
 * is expired from lack of activity.

# Javadoc Generation Summary: Kafka Record Batch Serialization

## Scope

Three classes in `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/` were documented:

| File | Type | Magic version |
|---|---|---|
| `RecordBatch.java` | Interface | v0 / v1 / v2 |
| `DefaultRecordBatch.java` | Concrete class | v2+ |
| `MemoryRecords.java` | Concrete class | all |

---

## RecordBatch.java

### Class-level Javadoc (replaced)
The original two-sentence description was expanded to cover:
- **Role in the Kafka protocol** — the interface is the fundamental unit of data transfer used by producers, brokers, and consumers.
- **Magic version history** — v0 (no timestamps), v1 (per-record timestamps, single-record batches without compression), v2 (batch header, delta encoding, CRC-32C, idempotent/transactional support).
- **Usage guidance** — when to prefer `streamingIterator` vs `iterator`, and the requirement to close the streaming iterator.
- **Thread-safety** — explicitly stated as *not thread-safe*; `DefaultRecordBatch` wraps a shared `ByteBuffer`.
- **`@see` cross-references** to `DefaultRecordBatch`, `MemoryRecords`, and `Record`.

### Methods improved
| Method | Change |
|---|---|
| `hasProducerId()` | Added `@return` tag (was missing) |
| `isCompressed()` | Expanded `@return`; added note on lazy vs eager decompression trade-off |
| `writeTo(ByteBuffer)` | Clarified buffer capacity requirement and position advance behaviour |
| `isTransactional()` | Added explanation of `read_committed` consumer isolation and `EndTransactionMarker` |
| `isControlBatch()` | Added examples of control records (transaction markers, KRaft metadata); noted v0/v1 behaviour |
| `offsetOfMaxTimestamp()` | Rewrote the ad-hoc comment block into standard Javadoc with `@return`, noted per-call decompression cost |

---

## DefaultRecordBatch.java

### Class-level Javadoc (replaced)
The existing detailed binary-format comment was restructured and significantly extended:
- Moved the wire schema into a `{@code}` pre-formatted block for Javadoc rendering.
- Added a **binary attributes table** in a `{@code}` block.
- Added dedicated **Compaction semantics**, **Timestamp semantics**, and **Performance** sections.
- Added **Thread-safety** statement: *not thread-safe*.
- Added `@see` references to `RecordBatch`, `MemoryRecords`, `MemoryRecordsBuilder`, and `DefaultRecord`.

### Methods documented (all previously undocumented public methods)
| Method | Key points documented |
|---|---|
| `writeTo(ByteBufferOutputStream)` | Buffer duplication (no mutation of internal state); may expand output stream |
| `recordInputStream(BufferSupplier)` | Lazy decompression; buffer pooling importance for LZ4; caller must close stream |
| `skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier)` | Memory-efficient alternative to `iterator()` for compressed batches; why uncompressed batches are unchanged; caller must close |
| `setLastOffset(long)` | Explains base-offset derivation from `lastOffsetDelta`; notes CRC is *not* recomputed; broker use case |
| `setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType, long)` | Describes early-exit optimisation; when CRC is recomputed; broker log-append-time use case |
| `setPartitionLeaderEpoch(int)` | Why CRC is not recomputed (field is excluded from CRC coverage) |
| `writeEmptyHeader(...)` | All 12 `@param` tags; broker compaction use case; `@throws` via delegation |
| `writeHeader(...)` | All 17 `@param` tags; CRC computation scope; called by `MemoryRecordsBuilder`; `@throws` for invalid magic/timestamp |
| `sizeInBytes(long, Iterable<Record>)` | Delta-encoding explanation; uncompressed/worst-case caveat |
| `sizeInBytes(Iterable<SimpleRecord>)` | Sequential offset-delta assignment; uncompressed caveat |
| `incrementSequence(int, int)` | Modular arithmetic wrap-around around `Integer.MAX_VALUE` |
| `decrementSequence(int, int)` | Inverse of `incrementSequence`; wrap-around below 0 |

---

## MemoryRecords.java

### Class-level Javadoc (replaced)
The three-line description was expanded to cover:
- **Role across producer/broker/consumer** stack.
- **Zero-copy serialization**, lazy batch iteration, filtering, and static factory methods.
- **Builder guidance** — when to use `MemoryRecordsBuilder` vs `readableRecords`.
- **Thread-safety** — *not thread-safe*; read-only concurrent access is safe provided `buffer()` is not mutated.
- `@see` references to `RecordBatch`, `DefaultRecordBatch`, `MemoryRecordsBuilder`, and `Records`.

### Methods documented (all previously undocumented public methods)
| Method | Key points documented |
|---|---|
| `sizeInBytes()` | Fixed at construction; reflects `ByteBuffer.limit()`; contrast with `validBytes()` |
| `writeTo(TransferableChannel, int, int)` | Zero-copy; partial-write semantics for non-blocking channels; `@throws` for out-of-range arguments |
| `batchIterator()` | Each call produces an independent iterator; lazy header parsing; no decompression |
| `buffer()` | Duplicate semantics (shared backing store, independent position/limit); read-only contract |
| `slice(int, int)` | No copy; clamping behaviour when `size` exceeds available bytes; `@throws` |
| `batches()` | Stateless iterable; multiple independent traversals; `@see batchIterator()` |
| `readableRecords(ByteBuffer)` | No copy; expected buffer state (position 0, limit = valid bytes) |
| `builder(ByteBuffer, Compression, TimestampType, long)` | Simple overload for non-idempotent producers; points to richer overloads |
| `withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)` | Single-buffer allocation; returns `EMPTY` for zero records |
| `withIdempotentRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...)` | Idempotent producer fields; duplicate detection |
| `withTransactionalRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...)` | Transactional flag; `read_committed` consumer behaviour |
| `withEndTransactionMarker(long, short, EndTransactionMarker)` | Control + transactional flags; commit/abort semantics |
| `writeEndTransactionalMarker(ByteBuffer, ...)` | In-place write variant; buffer capacity requirement |
| `withLeaderChangeMessage(...)` | KRaft `__cluster_metadata`; control flag; no compression |
| `withSnapshotHeaderRecord(...)` | KRaft snapshot start marker |
| `withSnapshotFooterRecord(...)` | KRaft snapshot end marker |
| `withKRaftVersionRecord(...)` | KRaft protocol version advertisement |
| `withVotersRecord(...)` | Raft quorum membership record |

---

## Quality checklist

| Requirement | Status |
|---|---|
| Thread-safety explicitly stated for every class | ✅ All three classes have explicit thread-safety guarantees |
| Performance implications noted for serialization/deserialization methods | ✅ `recordInputStream`, `skipKeyValueIterator`, `iterator`, `streamingIterator`, `sizeInBytes` variants |
| No Javadoc added to private methods | ✅ Only public methods documented |
| No internal/private fields documented | ✅ |
| Standard Javadoc tags used | ✅ `@param`, `@return`, `@throws`, `@see`, `{@link}`, `{@code}` |
| No implementation logic changed | ✅ |
| Cross-references using `{@link}` | ✅ Throughout all three files |

/** * A record batch is a container for one or more {@link Record} objects and is the fundamental unit of * data transfer in the Kafka wire protocol. Producers write batches to partition leaders; brokers * replicate, validate, and store them; consumers fetch and deserialize them. * *

The Kafka record format has evolved across three magic versions: *

    *
  • Magic v0 ({@link #MAGIC_VALUE_V0}) — original message format; no timestamps, no compression * across multiple messages in a single logical unit.
    • *
  • Magic v1 ({@link #MAGIC_VALUE_V1}) — added per-record timestamps and outer-record * compression, but a batch with no compression still contained exactly one record.
    • *
  • Magic v2 ({@link #MAGIC_VALUE_V2}, {@link #CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE}) — redesigned batch * format with a fixed batch header, variable-length delta-encoded records, efficient CRC-32C * checksumming, and first-class support for idempotent and transactional producers.
    • *
* *

Implementations carry read-only access to serialized bytes (e.g. a slice of a * {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer}). Callers should iterate records via {@link #iterator()} for simple * use cases or via {@link #streamingIterator(org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier)} for * memory-efficient streaming decompression. The streaming iterator must be closed after use. * *

Thread safety: Implementations of this interface are not guaranteed to be * thread-safe. {@link DefaultRecordBatch} instances wrap a shared {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer} and * must not be accessed concurrently from multiple threads without external synchronization. * * @see DefaultRecordBatch * @see MemoryRecords * @see Record */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

/** * Concrete {@link RecordBatch} implementation for magic version 2 and above (the current Kafka * record format). Instances wrap a read-only slice of a {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer} that holds the * complete, serialized representation of a single batch as it appears on the wire or on disk. * *

Binary layout

* 
{@code
 * RecordBatch =>
 *   BaseOffset            => Int64
 *   Length                => Int32
 *   PartitionLeaderEpoch  => Int32
 *   Magic                 => Int8
 *   CRC                   => Uint32
 *   Attributes            => Int16
 *   LastOffsetDelta       => Int32   // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *   BaseTimestamp         => Int64
 *   MaxTimestamp          => Int64
 *   ProducerId            => Int64
 *   ProducerEpoch         => Int16
 *   BaseSequence          => Int32
 *   RecordsCount          => Int32
 *   Records               => [Record]
 * }
* *

When compression is enabled, the compressed record bytes are serialized directly after * the {@code RecordsCount} field. The CRC covers every byte from {@code Attributes} to the * end of the batch using the CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial. The {@code PartitionLeaderEpoch} * field is intentionally excluded from the CRC to avoid recomputing it on every batch received * by the broker. * *

Batch attributes (bits 0–6 of the {@code Attributes} field)

* 
{@code
 * | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |
 * }
* *

Compaction semantics

* Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 preserves the first and last offset and sequence * numbers from the original batch through log compaction. This is necessary to correctly restore * idempotent producer state after a leader failure. If all records in a batch are removed during * compaction, the broker may retain an empty batch header (with {@code RecordsCount = 0}) to * preserve that producer sequence information. Empty batch headers are eventually removed once * the producer either writes a new sequence number or is expired due to inactivity. * *

Timestamp semantics

*
    *
  • {@code BaseTimestamp} normally reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch. * For an empty batch it is set to {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP}. When the * delete horizon flag is set, it holds the absolute wall-clock time at which * tombstone records and aborted-transaction markers in this batch should be purged.
    • *
  • {@code MaxTimestamp} holds the maximum timestamp across all current records when the * timestamp type is {@link org.apache.kafka.common.record.TimestampType#CREATE_TIME}. * For {@link org.apache.kafka.common.record.TimestampType#LOG_APPEND_TIME} it holds the * broker-assigned append time, which is preserved after compaction.
    • *
* *

Performance

* All fixed-width header fields are read directly from the backing {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer} * without copying. Iteration over uncompressed records is similarly zero-copy. Compressed records * are decoded lazily through {@link #streamingIterator(org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier)} * or eagerly (with full decompression into a temporary list) through {@link #iterator()}. * *

Thread safety

* This class is not thread-safe. Multiple threads must not read from or mutate (via * {@link MutableRecordBatch} methods) the same instance concurrently without external * synchronization. * * @see RecordBatch * @see MemoryRecords * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder * @see DefaultRecord */ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {

/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for
 * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and
 * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.
 *
 * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty
 * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches
 * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId
 * is expired from lack of activity.
 *
 * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp
 * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the
 * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp
 * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.
 *
 * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type
 * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set
 * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains
 * the previous value prior to becoming empty.
 *
 * The current attributes are given below:
 *
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 */
public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {

# Javadoc Generation Summary: Kafka Record Batch Serialization

## Scope

Three classes in `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/` were documented:

| File | Type | Magic version |
|---|---|---|

| `MemoryRecords.java` | Concrete class | all |

---

## RecordBatch.java

### Class-level Javadoc (replaced)
The original two-sentence description was expanded to cover:
- **Role in the Kafka protocol** — the interface is the fundamental unit of data transfer used by producers, brokers, and consumers.
- **Magic version history** — v0 (no timestamps), v1 (per-record timestamps, single-record batches without compression), v2 (batch header, delta encoding, CRC-32C, idempotent/transactional support).
- **Usage guidance** — when to prefer `streamingIterator` vs `iterator`, and the requirement to close the streaming iterator.
- **Thread-safety** — explicitly stated as *not thread-safe*; `DefaultRecordBatch` wraps a shared `ByteBuffer`.
- **`@see` cross-references** to `DefaultRecordBatch`, `MemoryRecords`, and `Record`.

### Methods improved
| Method | Change |
|---|---|
| `hasProducerId()` | Added `@return` tag (was missing) |
| `isCompressed()` | Expanded `@return`; added note on lazy vs eager decompression trade-off |
| `writeTo(ByteBuffer)` | Clarified buffer capacity requirement and position advance behaviour |
| `isTransactional()` | Added explanation of `read_committed` consumer isolation and `EndTransactionMarker` |
| `isControlBatch()` | Added examples of control records (transaction markers, KRaft metadata); noted v0/v1 behaviour |
| `offsetOfMaxTimestamp()` | Rewrote the ad-hoc comment block into standard Javadoc with `@return`, noted per-call decompression cost |

## DefaultRecordBatch.java

### Class-level Javadoc (replaced)
The existing detailed binary-format comment was restructured and significantly extended:
- Moved the wire schema into a `{@code}` pre-formatted block for Javadoc rendering.
- Added a **binary attributes table** in a `{@code}` block.
- Added dedicated **Compaction semantics**, **Timestamp semantics**, and **Performance** sections.
- Added **Thread-safety** statement: *not thread-safe*.
- Added `@see` references to `RecordBatch`, `MemoryRecords`, `MemoryRecordsBuilder`, and `DefaultRecord`.

### Methods documented (all previously undocumented public methods)
| Method | Key points documented |
|---|---|
| `writeTo(ByteBufferOutputStream)` | Buffer duplication (no mutation of internal state); may expand output stream |
| `recordInputStream(BufferSupplier)` | Lazy decompression; buffer pooling importance for LZ4; caller must close stream |
| `skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier)` | Memory-efficient alternative to `iterator()` for compressed batches; why uncompressed batches are unchanged; caller must close |
| `setLastOffset(long)` | Explains base-offset derivation from `lastOffsetDelta`; notes CRC is *not* recomputed; broker use case |
| `setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType, long)` | Describes early-exit optimisation; when CRC is recomputed; broker log-append-time use case |

| `withEndTransactionMarker(long, short, EndTransactionMarker)` | Control + transactional flags; commit/abort semantics |
| `writeEndTransactionalMarker(ByteBuffer, ...)` | In-place write variant; buffer capacity requirement |
